prevalence.trend: stable; absolute numbers rising with aging

burden.dalyPerCapita: quality-of-life impact from vasomotor and urogenital symptoms

epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: 12 months of amenorrhea after age 45; FSH testing if early or atypical

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: no screening; clinical diagnosis in primary care
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Moderate to high with access to counseling and hormone therapy

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Clinical diagnosis supported by labs when early or atypical
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Menopausal hormone therapy when eligible; non-hormonal therapies and lifestyle programs; vaginal estrogen for urogenital symptoms
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Variable access to specialist menopause services and concerns about HRT risks
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: EMAS guidelines, broad access to HRT, and structured counseling programs
